posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: Sauzet has gone from bad to excellent. Dauvissat does well. Nobody is perfect. I mentally add 30% to the price to arrive at an adjusted premox price and then decide if I should buy—I build in the anticipated premox. And I don’t buy as costly a wine, but that’s more because I can’t fathom 2018 pricing. Then I drink younger. All that mitigates premox.

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
Sauzet has gone from bad to excellent. Dauvissat does well. Nobody is perfect. I mentally add 30% to the price to arrive at an adjusted premox price and then decide if I should buy—I build in the anticipated premox. And I don’t buy as costly a wine, but that’s more because I can’t fathom 2018 pricing. Then I drink younger. All that mitigates premox.

I cannot bring myself to drink younger, but in all other respects agree 100% with this advice. Most of my white burgundy purchases now are my favorite premier crus where I can find and afford larger allocations, taking into account the expectation up to a third of the bottles may premox.

And to reiterate what others have said- there are no safe bets in this. Today’s poster child can become tomorrow’s leading premox disaster- Leflaive seems headed in that direction now. While things do seem to be slowly improving, from producer to producer it can still strike unexpectedly- and always years after the wines are sold.

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
I know the topic of premox has been discussed a million different ways and is covered in great detail between the white burg premox wiki and the Don Cornwell annual white burg vintage dinners. But after reading a few recent threads I wanted to ask the question in a slightly different way. If you were building a cellar of white burgs with the intent of seriously aging the wines and you were starting today (including some recent back vintages, but nothing super old), which 5-10 producers (or less) would be a must buy if your goal was to minimize premox risk and cost was not part of the equation?

You seek the Holy Grail. Are you worthy, Sir Galahad?

As others have stated, the longevity of White Burgundy is built on a base of ever shifting sand, so it’s almost impossible to predict which producers are going to give you the best results in 15-20 years. My money has gone into the producers who specialize in white burgundy, pick early, use old school presses, pay attention to every detail (including wax capsules for some), and have a recent track record of resisting pre-mox.

In my cellar, that translates into 5 producers that I bet on starting in 2004: Coche, Raveneau, PYCM, Dauvissat and Roulot. They are not infallible (especially Dauvissat), and I’ve only been cellaring them since 2004, so there’s only 12 vintages of data/experience (2004-2015). Based on CT consumption reports, my pre-mox/corked data from those producers has been:

Raveneau, 4 spoiled out of 88 consumed
PYCM, 3 spoiled out of 69 consumed
Roulot, 2 spoiled out of 66 consumed
Dauvissat, 7 spoiled out of 63 consumed
Coche, 0 spoiled out of 51 consumed

I no longer buy Dauvissat because of pre-mox experience, and I can’t get Coche, Raveneau and Roulot any more from my long time retailer who changed hands and the allocations didn’t transfer to the new owner (thanks Kermit). Prices are insane on those producers in the secondary market.

To conclude, my advice is if you have the money and the access, cellar Coche, Raveneau, Roulot and PYCM.

posts.name: Robert Grenley

I’d be a bit wary of Raveneau if I were you.

These bottles were recently pulled from an underground bonded warehouse in the UK. They’d only ever travelled from the Domaine to the cellar.

The picture demonstrates the random nature of premox.

The wine is the 1999 Butteaux

I like your Premox Detection Chamber. I need one!

Others have said it - but to cellar I buy only in DIAM. Though I’ll happily buy - to drink - wine sealed with anything, so long as it’s less than 3 years old. I expect that screw-cap could also be as good, but in Burgundy their market penetration is tiny - so you can forget finding important wines with that seal - everything is available with DIAM…

And for all the urban myth peddlers, there is no producer that is immune to oxidation and other faults, though some certainly have fewer problems. Wine sealed with cork can be great - obviously! - but avove a certain age, not on my dollar (any more) and it is (at best) variable, and subject to multiple potential taints - not just TCA which some suppliers now offer a guarantee against…

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
Obviously, people are going to suggest Coche and Raveneau and Roulot though that may not be a lot of help to anyone starting out collecting WB who lacks $quillions or long-standing connections to those with serious allocations. I would suggest:

  • Jacques Carillon, particularly at village and 1er levels I’ve had no issue across dozens and dozens (probably hundreds) of bottles
  • Leflaive, now with Diam. I’ve not had a lot in recent years but tried a beautiful '14 Combettes recently that makes me optimistic
  • Bouchard, who really do do a good range
  • Sauzet, others have said they seem to have very few premox issues now
    Then, if you really must slum it with the occasional wine from the ‘lesser’ regions like Meursault and Chablis I’m sure you can find a couple of names to round things out

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I wonder if we are going to start seeing oxidized coche with the regime change. Seems to have far less sulfur / matchstick on the nose now.

Raphael is still pressing like his father, with lots of dry extract in the wines, and the élevage is just as detail-oriented, so is the bottling. I am not too concerned on that front.

I think the idea that overt reduction is the key to avoiding premox is a blind alley. Have seen plenty of white Burgundies go from reduced to oxidized with nothing in between, and the great wines of René Lafon and plenty of others were not reduced and aged very well.

Threads on premox tend to go off on tangents, often devolving into debates about screw caps, with everyone in search of one single cause, when the reality is complex and multifactorial but not so mysterious. Picking date, fruit quality, pressing (how hard, how long), élevage (how much lees, how much stirring and when, how much new oak, potential use of clarification enzymes, cellar temperature, barrel topping, sulfur additions and other factors), bottling (sulfur levels, dissolved oxygen, bottling practices) and closures are all part of a complex system. Some of the explanations are to do with technical mistakes (e.g. picking up too much dissolved oxygen in racking tanks and bottling lines) but others are to do with style and intention: no one really wants to wait a decade on Bourgogne Blanc and village wines and two for premiers crus, and producers understand this. Moreover, in the premox era our collective palate is changing: most of us would find Pierre Ramonet’s wines of the '70s and '80s far too austere and structural if we went back in time to taste them from barrel today.

Why do you think this all came together in a perfect storm in 1995 and 1996 (and of course thereafter)? It seems bizarre that almost everyone’s wines became affected at the same time.

My question said differently was whether white burgs are less ageable today than they used to be simply because they were made to hit their optimal drinking windows more quickly than the older white burgs referenced by William (i.e. Ramonet’s from the 70s and 80s). The answers I got from both you and William suggest that someone looking to build a collection of seriously aged white burgs starting today would be contending with both premox and wines that are made to peak sooner.

posts.name: Andrew Hamilton
posts.cooked:
Moreover, in the premox era our collective palate is changing: most of us would find Pierre Ramonet’s wines of the '70s and '80s far too austere and structural if we went back in time to taste them from barrel today.

Does this statement imply that maybe white burgs today are not as ageable as they once were even when we put premox aside?

I think it’s a strong possibility. By all accounts, ageing many of the white Burgundies of yesteryear wasn’t a choice but a necessity. Tasting, e.g. the 1988 whites today, that’s easy to believe.

I find these comments curious for a couple reasons. Firstly because in broad brush strokes they mirror what Galloni says about Barolo. Specifically his assertion that historically young Barolo was virtually undrinkable due to tannin and acidity.

Secondly I find in curious because some things are described as stylistic shifts eg the shift to elegance in Burgundy, which is described as a change in style but not necessarily a wholesale change of the wines categorically across the board. Conversely White Burgs and Barolo seem to be more readily described by some as having undergone huge changes that make comparison to the same wine from earlier vintages impossible. That then implies that their ageing profile would also change.

So I guess what I’m trying to get at is I don’t really understand where the tipping point lies between a “stylistic shift” like red Burgundy to a complete change in the wines comparable to white burgundy and the woes regarding premox.

I’m not expecting any answers and I expect as with most things a “real” answer would be very detailed, granular, and a case by case basis. Just thought it an interesting comparison to make.

As to OPs question, I myself am putting my trust in Bouchard. Great wines under DIAM 10. I’ve also just had a look and apparently DIAM 30 is now on the market? Anyone seen one of those “in the wild” yet?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I wonder if we are going to start seeing oxidized coche with the regime change. Seems to have far less sulfur / matchstick on the nose now.

Raphael is still pressing like his father, with lots of dry extract in the wines, and the élevage is just as detail-oriented, so is the bottling. I am not too concerned on that front.

I think the idea that overt reduction is the key to avoiding premox is a blind alley. Have seen plenty of white Burgundies go from reduced to oxidized with nothing in between, and the great wines of René Lafon and plenty of others were not reduced and aged very well.

Threads on premox tend to go off on tangents, often devolving into debates about screw caps, with everyone in search of one single cause, when the reality is complex and multifactorial but not so mysterious. Picking date, fruit quality, pressing (how hard, how long), élevage (how much lees, how much stirring and when, how much new oak, potential use of clarification enzymes, cellar temperature, barrel topping, sulfur additions and other factors), bottling (sulfur levels, dissolved oxygen, bottling practices) and closures are all part of a complex system. Some of the explanations are to do with technical mistakes (e.g. picking up too much dissolved oxygen in racking tanks and bottling lines) but others are to do with style and intention: no one really wants to wait a decade on Bourgogne Blanc and village wines and two for premiers crus, and producers understand this. Moreover, in the premox era our collective palate is changing: most of us would find Pierre Ramonet’s wines of the '70s and '80s far too austere and structural if we went back in time to taste them from barrel today.

.
Why do you think this all came together in a perfect storm in 1995 and 1996 (and of course thereafter)? It seems bizarre that almost everyone’s wines became affected at the same time.

A lot of the winemaking practices that are likely culpable (lots of new wood, battonage, using mechanical bottling lines instead of bottling barrel-by-barrel by hand in the cellar) were being widely adopted then, add to which a nadir for closures and a vintage amazingly high in malic acid that would naturally have produced very taut, long-lived wines that needed a long time in barrel to process their malic acid and clarify. Producers wanted them to taste more approachable young and to get them to market sooner, so they heated cellars (Arnaud Ente remembers sweating in the cellar in 1996) and stirred like crazy. I suspect plenty used clarification enzymes and filtered to get the wines clear and in bottle. When you add terrible corks—in its own right a sufficient explanation in some cases—into the mix 1996 makes sense.

And it’s worth adding that consumers’ high expectations for the longevity of the 1996 white Burgundies likely threw the problems into heightened relief.

posts.name: Andrew Hamilton
posts.cooked:
As to OPs question, I myself am putting my trust in Bouchard. Great wines under DIAM 10.

Had the 2014 Meursault Perrieres from Bouchard last night - boy was that good.

It’s SO good. If anything the '14 Bouchard Corton Charlemagne is even better.

Howard, that '14 MP from Bouchard is an absolute RIPPER. It’s an annual 3 pack purchase for me, and if I’m being honest it’s my #1 WB when you take cost into consideration.

Ryan, you’re also correct regarding the CC. Definitely a step up from the MP 1er, especially in concentration/density on the palate. I don’t pick one up every year as IIRC it’s nearly twice the cost of the MP but I still try and snag a single bottle with each vintage. For me it’s the best QPR GC WB I have access to annually from an allocation perspective.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Howard, that '14 MP from Bouchard is an absolute RIPPER. It’s an annual 3 pack purchase for me, and if I’m being honest it’s my #1 WB when you take cost into consideration.

As I have stated elsewhere, I think Bouchard and Drouhin are the two most underrated producers of white Burgundy out there. It is interesting. When I first started drinking Burgundy in the late 70s and early 80s, the big producers were overrated and most thought that smaller producers (with a few exceptions) could not compete with them. So, in the 80s, many, many smaller producer wines were way underpriced compared with the wines from the big guys and were real bargains (think Champagne today in many cases). Now, things have entirely flipped. People underrate the wines from the best large producers and they are relative bargains in the world of Burgundy - and findable.

posts.name: Y Edan
posts.cooked:
In my cellar, that translates into 5 producers that I bet on starting in 2004: Coche, Raveneau, PYCM, Dauvissat and Roulot. They are not infallible (especially Dauvissat), and I’ve only been cellaring them since 2004, so there’s only 12 vintages of data/experience (2004-2015). Based on CT consumption reports, my pre-mox/corked data from those producers has been:

Raveneau, 4 spoiled out of 88 consumed
PYCM, 3 spoiled out of 69 consumed
Roulot, 2 spoiled out of 66 consumed
Dauvissat, 7 spoiled out of 63 consumed
Coche, 0 spoiled out of 51 consumed

I no longer buy Dauvissat because of pre-mox experience, and I can’t get Coche, Raveneau and Roulot any more from my long time retailer who changed hands and the allocations didn’t transfer to the new owner (thanks Kermit). Prices are insane on those producers in the secondary market.

To conclude, my advice is if you have the money and the access, cellar Coche, Raveneau, Roulot and PYCM.



Obviously, people are going to suggest Coche and Raveneau and Roulot though that may not be a lot of help to anyone starting out collecting WB who lacks $quillions or long-standing connections to those with serious allocations.

Larry, that’s pretty solid data on Roulot. I’ve heard in other threads that people have noticed an uptick in premox with Roulot, but the only two mentions of Roulot in this thread (quoted above) are incredibly positive. Any other views on Roulot, particularly as the price has gone up dramatically over the last few years?

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
There was not a wholesale shift in white Burgundy styles between 1993 and 1995 or 1996- that is a convenient myth to try and find a reason for the advent of premox. As a matter of fact, the 1982, 1983, 1985, 1986 and 1989 vintages of white Burgundy were every bit as enjoyable to drink right out of the blocks as the 1995s and even more enjoyable than the 1996s at the outset. Yes, SO2 levels were higher in the '80s than was the case in 1996, but vignerons raised their SO2 levels once premox became an ongoing concern in the early vintages of the 2000s, without meaningful success, so SO2 levels were not the cause. Only the 1988 whites were not tasty out of the blocks- they were green, grassy, acidic and seemingly completely devoid of sufficient ripeness to make an enjoyable glass anytime in their future. Their metamorphosis into outstanding white wines is one of the biggest surprises of the last quarter century in Burgundy. When they were first released they were almost impossible to sell and lingered on shelves for years.

Similar changes in terms of type of press, use of enzymes, battonage (first widely used in the late '80s in vintages that did not premox), more new oak etc, cited by William above, were all transitioning in from the late 1980s onward; they do not satisfactorily explain why the 1993s aged fine and the 1995 vintage forward premoxed. Cork changes, such as rinsing in peroxide, first began at Amorim in 1989 and were used consistently from that time forward, so again, we have vintages with these corks that aged just fine and later vintages that did not. Similarly, global warming really got started in the late 1980s, but vintages up through 1995 (or perhaps 1994 was the real start of premox in Burgundy, as it was with white Bordeaux) aged what we would term “classically” and those from 1995 forwards premoxed. It does not make sense to look for the cause of premox in what the Burgundians changed between 1993 and 1995, because the answer is, wholesale, nothing changed at the flip of a switch. The potential causes William cited were all, to varying degrees, transitioning in over an extended period of ten or more vintages here, and yet we have a cut off between long-term cellaring potential with the 1993 vintage back, and premox from 1995 forwards. Sure, they could all have potential impacts to diminish the ability of white Burgundy to age long-term, but they do not explain the chasm between 1993s aging and nothing since. Additionally, premox is not an exclusively Burgundian experience, as the 1994 vintage was great for white Bordeaux and that vintage also premoxed. Alsace wines premox. Savennieres premoxes. It is not just white Burgundy and white Bordeaux. Certainly some shared techniques could be pinpointed between white Burgundy and white Bordeaux in the mid-1990s, but again, not techniques that changed wholesale from one vintage to the next. And yet, 1993 white Bordeaux and back is still cruising along just fine, and 1994 forwards premoxes.

To my knowledge, what has not really been looked at closely (though there may be ongoing research I am unaware of) is the quality of the bottles themselves. If the interior of the necks are pebbled from poor manufacturing (according to Tim Frohlich of Weingut Schafer-Frohlich in the Nahe, this is indeed the case and he has done research on his own bottles in this regard, due to some of his Grosses Gewachs bottles premoxing as well eight to ten years out from the vintage), then there is not going to be a clean, tight and anaerobic interface between the outside of the cork and the interior of the neck of the bottle. Such a condition would provide the avenue for oxygen ingress and also explain the bottle by bottle phenomenon of premox. If this is indeed the case, then one can go back to see if vignerons opted to buy their bottles somewhere different in the 1993 and 1995 vintages, or specs on bottle manufacturing may have been relaxed by the glass companies. These possibilities seem far more likely to have occurred on a more universal scale than such things as battonage, bladder presses, enzymes etc, which would have been adopted or not on an individual basis, rather than universally in the 1994 or 1995 vintages. Solving the problem of premox of white Burgundy (and white Bordeaux, as I love those wines at full maturity as well) would be a great, great improvement. But, it is still not solved, and until that time, building a cellar of white Burgundy for long-term cellaring remains seriously problematic, unless the casualty rate from premox does not bother you.

Thank you for the response, this is incredibly helpful and thoughtful. Unfortunately the conclusion is a bit depressing in the context of my OP. If it is a bottle issue (or some more universal problem), why have some producers like Coche largely avoided premox?

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
John, I have long thought the problem was the “seal”. There are two parts to that: the cork and the bottle neck. And, you have focused apprarently bottle neck.

Since discussions with several “thinking” white wine makes in Burgundy a decade or go, I have focused on the corks and their rigidity, and, therefore, inability to make as good a seal as in the past. (Whether the bottle itself plays a role could be a “chicken and egg” issue.) The corks certainly seem to have become more rigid within the suspect period. Some “research” I did and some you tube videos pointed me to the addition of silicone as creating more rigid corks (that’s what the winemakers suggested in 20017). But…now I’m thinking the issue might even be simpler and easier to point to. In “that” period, the cork makers, deciding that their biggest problem for sales were spoiled corks, decided that drying them out was the antidote to cork/wine flaws from the corks. I think they’ve carried it to far…and basically, what’s been inserted into bottles (randomly, as corks are all variable) has essentially been like a dry sponge…unable to make a good seal in random cases.

The bottle theory is interesting…but…the variability of the plague within a given group points, to me, to variability of something…and…focusing on the bad good bottles, I am skeptical that bottle manufacturing suddenly became more variable from bottle to bottle, sufficient to cause the “premox”.

I would love to find out the evolution and chronology of the cork industry’s efforts at drying the corks to prevent mold/TCA…and when they changed what in doing them. Of course, the “cork industry” is many companies…and just the amount of drying could be the variable.

FWIW

p.s. Do you know anything about the purported sale of the Robert/Michel Ampeau estate someone here implied…?

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Moreover, in the premox era our collective palate is changing: most of us would find Pierre Ramonet’s wines of the '70s and '80s far too austere and structural if we went back in time to taste them from barrel today.

Does this statement imply that maybe white burgs today are not as ageable as they once were even when we put premox aside?

I think it’s a strong possibility. By all accounts, aging many of the white Burgundies of yesteryear wasn’t a choice but a necessity. Tasting, e.g. the 1988 whites today, that’s easy to believe.

I completely agree. White Burgs today seem much riper and less acidic than they were before premox. It would make sense that they are less resistant to premox (even if there are one or more other culprits).

Along with the risk of failure, it seems like fewer and fewer White Burgs really demand extended aging. Though I don’t buy the grand crus and trophies, so maybe those still do.

posts.cooked:
How do you account for the randomness/variability in an given group of wines, Chris…?

I realilze you are not saying that the riper/less acidic wines , themselves, account for the premox…but, I consider the “premox” the bad bottles. The others are fine…though might become oxidized.

posts.name: J0hn $mith
posts.cooked:
If it is a bottle issue (or some more universal problem), why have some producers like Coche largely avoided premox?

Good point. Hard to see the bottle or cork theory hold true. Coche, Roulot or Raveneau magically got the perfect bottles or corks is silly. The other explanation is Coche and Roulot are equally affected. The sample size is too small or people unwilling to admit the expensive wine they bought was oxidized.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
People say that is the case…, but…I have no idea it is accurate/ I’ve had bad Roulot.

Maybe they soak the corks to add moisture before bottling??

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
If it is a bottle issue (or some more universal problem), why have some producers like Coche largely avoided premox?

Good point. Hard to see the bottle or cork theory hold true. Coche, Roulot or Raveneau magically got the perfect bottles or corks is silly. The other explanation is Coche and Roulot are equally affected. The sample size is too small or people unwilling to admit the expensive wine they bought was oxidized.

Hi John,

I have had premoxed bottles from all three producers- the account that they are immune is an urban legend. So have many other people I know with broad experience with these producers. No one is immune. As both Roulot and Coche bottled their wines far more reductively for many vintages (perhaps with a goal of holding off the onset of premox), I would guess that this is one reason they are often cited as immune. But, too much reduction risks killing the wine by getting stuck in a permanently reduced state in the bottle and not developing with bottle age. William Kelley and I shared a bottle of Roulot 2004 last year that was permanently reduced and stuck in neutral for the rest of its life. So going for a far more reductive state prior to bottling is its own problem, even if it holds off premox. I just had four 1996 Domaine Leflaive wines that had also been bottled very reductively; none had premoxed, but all four were not perfect either. The Chevalier had blossomed properly and was still fresh and complex (not to mention extremely enjoyable), but the Pucelles, Combettes and Batard were all permanently reduced to varying degrees and again had not developed any bottle complexity as a result. Maybe they will one day (or maybe there are bottles out there that are less reduced), but my gut feeling was that they were permanently stuck in adolescence. I note that I have already tasted at both Domaines Roulot and Coche-Dury on this trip and their 2017s and 2016s respectively seem far less reductive than was the case four or five vintages ago, so perhaps the producers have also had disappointing bottle development over the recent past and are backing off from this strategy?

All the Best,

John

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
Hi Stuart,

I have not noticed that corks are drier now than before premox, but have not thought of this possibility either. Silicone is not the cause though, as both Comtes Lafon and Joseph Drouhin (among others) thought to eliminate silicone coatings back a decade ago, hoping that using the paraffin to coat the corks that was used before silicone (and before premox) would give a better cork-bottle interface. But, it was not successful at holding off premox and I believe that both estates have gone now to a mix of paraffin and silicone to coat the corks, as pure paraffin makes a young cork extremely difficult to pull from the bottle (and this is not an ideal thing in an era where so many have to drink our white Burgs younger now).

If you want to find out when the drying of the corks changed (or if it did) and over what timeframe, contact Amorim’s media relations arm. I did this when I thought that peroxide rinsing could be the cause of premox and they were extremely helpful and generous with their time and their data. I have little doubt that they would be upfront about what changed and when, if this was indeed the case. Amorim would love to help solve the problem and come up with a permanent solution, as it would be a PR coup for them. So contact them and see if your theories are correct, as it is an interesting angle to explore.

All the Best,

John

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Thanks, John. I just sent them an email to their general “information” address for "communications.

I used to think the silicone had a role in rigidity of the corks, but I think that is not the case. I know that kiln-drying has made the corks more rigid…and I do wonder when that started, and, particularly, how much is too much/ how they can be consistent within any given batch of corks, since corks are not uniform themselves.

Stuart

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: John, I remember when Parker gave huge scores to 2002 Burgs from Verget. They were stirring their lees and doing things to make the wines more accessible early. It has always seemed interesting that premox started soon after these scores came out. Are you sure producers did not start doing things then (maybe even picking later) to make wines more accessible to get higher scores? Some may not admit to making changes.

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
John, I remember when Parker gave huge scores to 2002 Burgs from Verget. They were stirring their lees and doing things to make the wines more accessible early. It has always seemed interesting that premox started soon after these scores came out. Are you sure producers did not start doing things then (maybe even picking later) to make wines more accessible to get higher scores? Some may not admit to making changes.

Hi Howard,

Premox started long before Parker gave big scores to the Verget 2002s, so his scores were not a contributory factor in premox. Jean-Marie Guffens was a huge proponent of battoange with his Verget bottlings, but I think the earliest high profile producer using lots of battonage was Dominique Lafon, who used this a lot starting in 1989 and all through the first half of the 1990s. But, as I noted above, his wines prior to 1995 aged fine, those past premoxed. Certainly battonage, particularly when the casks are opened to allow the stirring (rather than rolling the barrels with the fine lees still in them, which some started doing a bit later) introduces more oxygen into the wines and contributes a bit to potential longevity being shortened, but it is not a factor in premox, as battonage does not account for the significant bottle by bottle variation that is emblematic of premox.

Robert Parker’s influence on the Burgundy region diminished dramatically after he lost his libel suit to Francois Faiveley and stopped visiting the region, which was back in the mid-1990s. He was never as influential in Burgundy as in other regions in any case, but his presence in the Burgundy market was the least visible of any major region (other than maybe Germany and the Loire) during the time period you cite. So, while perhaps some producers might have tweaked things in a certain direction to get higher Parker scores, other than Bobby Kacher’s portfolio of domaines, there were not all that many cuvees made to cater to his palate preferences. Certainly white Burgundy producers such as Ramonet, Sauzet, Jadot, Colin-Deleger and the like, who were some of the most prominent early victims of premox, would have changed absolutely nothing in their regimen to cater to Monsieur Parker’s palate, as they did not need to, with more demand than bottles for all of their white wines at the time.

All the Best,

John

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
John, I remember when Parker gave huge scores to 2002 Burgs from Verget. They were stirring their lees and doing things to make the wines more accessible early. It has always seemed interesting that premox started soon after these scores came out. Are you sure producers did not start doing things then (maybe even picking later) to make wines more accessible to get higher scores? Some may not admit to making changes.

Hi Howard,

Premox started long before Parker gave big scores to the Verget 2002s, so his scores were not a contributory factor in premox. Jean-Marie Guffens was a huge proponent of battoange with his Verget bottlings, but I think the earliest high profile producer using lots of battonage was Dominique Lafon, who used this a lot starting in 1989 and all through the first half of the 1990s. But, as I noted above, his wines prior to 1995 aged fine, those past premoxed. Certainly battonage, particularly when the casks are opened to allow the stirring (rather than rolling the barrels with the fine lees still in them, which some started doing a bit later) introduces more oxygen into the wines and contributes a bit to potential longevity being shortened, but it is not a factor in premox, as battonage does not account for the significant bottle by bottle variation that is emblematic of premox.

John

The potential oxidative impact of battonage is that is de-gases wine that would otherwise be protected by its charge of carbon dioxide. While malolactic fermentation is still going on and the wine’s charge of CO2 is being replenished, battonage actually promotes reduction as the lees scavenge oxygen. So there is a big difference between doing battonage before and after the conclusion of malolactic fermentation.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
John, I remember when Parker gave huge scores to 2002 Burgs from Verget. They were stirring their lees and doing things to make the wines more accessible early. It has always seemed interesting that premox started soon after these scores came out. Are you sure producers did not start doing things then (maybe even picking later) to make wines more accessible to get higher scores? Some may not admit to making changes.

The 2002 scores were from Pierre Rovani, in fact - and some of the wines are aging very well. The Guffens-Heynen 2002s I’ve had are all impeccable, and the 2002 Verget Puligny Enseignères was still tangy and youthful from a bottle this year. Bob covered these wines through the early-1990s, but not after.

Guffens himself thinks that wines being fatigued by excessive time in new wood, plus poor closures, is the cause of premox - which may well be true of his wines. Certainly, now that he has backed off from new wood for Verget and uses DIAM and screwcap, I haven’t heard of or experienced, any problems.

posts.cooked: Great info, William!

posts.name: Don Cornwell
posts.cooked:

Are you sure producers did not start doing things then (maybe even picking later) to make wines more accessible to get higher scores? Some may not admit to making changes.

Hi Howard,

Premox started long before Parker gave big scores to the Verget 2002s, so his scores were not a contributory factor in premox. Jean-Marie Guffens was a huge proponent of battoange with his Verget bottlings, but I think the earliest high profile producer using lots of battonage was Dominique Lafon, who used this a lot starting in 1989 and all through the first half of the 1990s. But, as I noted above, his wines prior to 1995 aged fine, those past premoxed. Certainly battonage, particularly when the casks are opened to allow the stirring (rather than rolling the barrels with the fine lees still in them, which some started doing a bit later) introduces more oxygen into the wines and contributes a bit to potential longevity being shortened, but it is not a factor in premox, as battonage does not account for the significant bottle by bottle variation that is emblematic of premox.

The potential oxidative impact of battonage is that is de-gases wine that would otherwise be protected by its charge of carbon dioxide. While malolactic fermentation is still going on and the wine’s charge of CO2 is being replenished, battonage actually promotes reduction as the lees scavenge oxygen. So there is a big difference between doing battonage before and after the conclusion of malolactic fermentation.

There appears to be a historical inaccuracy here. Mr. Parker went nuts for the 1992 vintage of Verget, not 2002. Parker outwardly praised producers who claimed to use little or no SO2 in their wines (like Verget and later Jadot) and he lauded praise on those who used extensive lees stirring. Guffens at that time claimed that using extensive batonnage protected the wine from oxidation. He later publicly admitted that he was wrong. By way of background, there were also a lot of purported health nuts at that time (I emphasize the nuts part) who strongly campaigned for banning the use of sulfites in food and beverages, or for requiring warning labels on food and wines, including the warning that wines contains sulfites. The fact that people continue to promote so-called “natural” wines that contain no (added) SO2 continues to confound me. Editorializing aside, a lot of burgundy producers in the mid 90s cut the amount of free SO2 that the wines were bottled with, and at the same time dramatically increased batonnage. Added to the big problems that came from the declining quality of the corks, it was the perfect storm for premox.

With respect to CO2, it’s only role in preventing premox is to act as a physical barrier to oxidation when the wine is undergoing malolactic fermentation in a closed barrel environment. CO2 does not in any way chemically prevent or retard oxidation. There are two producers who claim that they can substitute CO2 for SO2 to prevent oxidation (Fourrier and LeMoine). This is absolutely wrong from a science standpoint and it is a little bit like believing that the earth is flat. [If you haven’t already noticed, you’ll find that the reds from both producers age notably faster than their counterparts from the same vintages. The LeMoine grand crus tasted at 10 years are consistently the most developed/advanced wines of the vintage and you can usually drink them at that stage while many other wines are still at least five years away. The whites from LeMoine are ticking time bombs that I completely avoid.]

William is correct that opening the barrel to perform batonnage tends to purge some of the CO2 in the barrel and allows oxygen to enter – and it thus promotes oxidation. Performing post ML batonnage, which some producers used to do, is a prescription for disaster unless SO2 is being added at the same time. If the lees are left in the barrel, they promote reduction because they scavenge oxygen and while M/L is ongoing the wine is releasing CO2 into the barrel. While stirring of the lees (by rolling closed barrels) may accelerate the lees’ oxygen scavenging, how much that helps is really a matter of conjecture. The producers are not performing the batonnage to increase reduction in the wine, but rather on the theory that the stimulating greater mixing of the wine with its lees “enriches” the flavor elements of the wine.

For those of you who weren’t aware of it, our former host for the wiki site shut down their hosting operations for educational sites. Guillaume Deschamps, who created the wiki site, moved it to his own file server. You will find it here: start [Oxidized Burgundies]

posts.name: Don Cornwell
posts.cooked:
In response to the original question, I think cost is always a factor. Also, Bill and Alan are right that DIAM changes the calculation (and the best bets to buy once the producers in question switched to DIAM.)

Based on their historical performance through the 2010 vintage, here’s my list of the top producers to buy based upon minimizing premox without regard to cost:

Absolute Lowest Premox Incidence: These producers have no greater incidence of premox in the vintages after 1994 than they did in the vintages before 1994: Coche-Dury, DRC, Leroy/D’Auvenay and Raveneau. [One cautionary note here. There have been some changes at Coche-Dury since Raphael took over and I’m not positive that the wines will be as premox resistant after 2009 as they were previously. Time will tell.]

Highly Recommended and Below Average Premox Incidence: This group of producers have lower than average incidence of premature oxidation. In some cases, the producer in question has had oxidation problems in only one or two particular vintages since 1995: Bouchard [incidence was low from 2000 to 2008, and in 2009 switched to DIAM]; Jacques Carillon [since 2002 only - at one time a high incidence producer], Colin-Morey, Bernard Moreau, Marc Morey, Jean-Marc Pillot [since 2002 – another former high incidence producer], Ramonet grand crus [since 2001], and Roulot.

Serious producers whose wines now have to be considered again because of their switch to DIAM: Chablis: Fevre (beginning 2010) and Droin (staring with 2011). Cote de Beaune: Montille and Chateau de Puligny Montrachet (starting with 2009), Jadot (starting with 2011), Lafon (starting with 2013), and Domaine Leflaive (starting with 2014).

posts.name: Herwig Janssen
posts.cooked:
This is a really interesting discussion . Don , fwiw , I had more than a few premoxed Montrachet’s from DRC , including some opened by Bernard Noblet at the Domaine . ( try the 2005 now… )
My small contribution to the discussion is this : there is a difference between premature aging and just aging . For example : the 2001’s from Coche Dury were fantastic wine , outclassing the vintage which for white Burgundy is nothing special . But starting about 5 years ago , they entered the stage of decline . They are no longer great . This is not premox but normal aging . Not all vintages age the same way . Too many times , aging is confused with premox .

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I heard something at a small winery in Israel that has me concerned. They told me that some part of the US government is at least considering banning DIAM because of some chemical in DIAM. I hope that this is not true. Does anyone know anything about this?

posts.name: Don Cornwell
posts.cooked:
I heard something at a small winery in Israel that has me concerned. They told me that some part of the US government is at least considering banning DIAM because of some chemical in DIAM. I hope that this is not true. Does anyone know anything about this?

Howard:

I haven’t heard anything about it. It could be propaganda because the binder material is FDA approved. According to an article in the Drinks Business in 2017, DIAM uses acrylate microparticles to fill the tiny air spaces in the agglomerated closure, along with a polyurethane glue to bind the ground cork. It was approved by the FDA. In 2017, DIAM began offering a new closure called “Origine” which substitutes a beeswax emulsion and a binder composed of 100% vegetable polyols. They claim that there is no difference in oxygen transmission rates and the alternative versions are offered as either a Diam 10 and Diam 30. Here is an article which explains the new version of the DIAM closures. Diam launches agglomerated cork with plant-based binder - The Drinks Business They are about 33% more expensive, but apparently there have been lots of orders. The company is strongly denying that there is any health risk or any type of chemical leeching from regular DIAM closures.

link_counts.title: Diam launches agglomerated cork with plant-based binder - The Drinks Business
